title: Graph Theory and Applications in Social Work
description: Master graph theory concepts through social work applications, from family network mapping to organizational partnership analysis. An adaptive course that adjusts to your skill level.

nodes.title: Understanding Networks in Social Work
nodes.content: Graphs are visual representations of relationships and connections. In social work, we use graphs to map client support networks, interagency collaboration, and service delivery systems. Each person, organization, or service is a 'node,' and each relationship or connection is an 'edge.' By understanding network structure, social workers can identify isolated clients, key community resources, and bottlenecks in service delivery. This course teaches you to apply graph theory to real social work challenges, from mapping family systems to analyzing organizational partnerships.

nodes.title: Basic Graph Concepts in Social Work
nodes.content: Test your understanding of fundamental graph theory concepts applied to social work practice.

questions.question: A client's support network includes: the client (node), their family members (nodes), and their connections to a counselor, church group, and food bank (edges). How many edges does this represent?
feedback: No, there are more connections than that.
Almost—count the client's connections to external resources.
Correct! The client has edges to three external entities.
Remember, edges connect nodes; family members are separate nodes.
questions.explanation: The three edges represent the client's connections to three distinct resources or support systems.

questions.question: A social service agency network has 4 nodes: Health Center, Mental Health Clinic, Job Training, and Housing Services. If each service refers clients to every other service, how many edges (two-way referral relationships) exist?
feedback: That's the number of nodes.
Correct! 4 agencies with mutual referrals create 6 connections.
Consider that each connection is counted once, not twice.
That would count bidirectional separately.
questions.explanation: With 4 nodes in a fully connected network, the number of edges is 4×3÷2 = 6 (combination formula).

questions.question: In a family genogram (family tree used in social work), what represents an 'edge'?
options: Relationships between family members (marriage, parent-child, sibling)
feedback: Those are nodes.
Correct! Edges represent the connections between family members.
That's the entire graph.
While related to social work, edges are formal connections.
questions.explanation: In graph terminology, edges are the connections or relationships between entities (nodes), not the entities themselves.

nodes.title: Advanced Network Analysis in Social Work
nodes.content: Test your understanding of more sophisticated graph analysis concepts and their application to social work challenges.

questions.question: A social worker is mapping referral pathways for a homeless client. The fastest path from the Emergency Shelter to permanent housing involves: Shelter → Mental Health Services → Job Training → Housing. What does graph theory call this?
feedback: A cycle would loop back to the start.
Correct! This is the shortest path through the network.
A tree has no cycles; this may have alternative routes.
A complete graph connects every node to every other node.
questions.explanation: A shortest path is the most efficient route between two nodes, a key concept for designing service pathways.

questions.question: In an organization's collaboration network, the CEO is connected to every department head, and each department head is connected to their team members. What network position gives the CEO the most influence?
feedback: Betweenness measures how often someone bridges others; the CEO doesn't bridge departments.
Correct! The CEO has the highest degree centrality (most direct connections).
Closeness measures average distance to all nodes; the CEO is far from team members.
Eigenvector considers the quality of connections, not quantity.
questions.explanation: Degree centrality measures the number of direct connections—the CEO's many direct connections make them influential.

questions.question: An organization wants to identify which community partner is most critical to their referral network. They should analyze:
options: The partner with the most clients
The partner's betweenness centrality (how often they bridge other organizations)
The geographic location of all partners
feedback: More clients doesn't mean more network influence.
Correct! High betweenness means they bridge different parts of the network.
Geography is important but not a graph theory measure.
Cost efficiency is important but not a network position measure.
questions.explanation: Betweenness centrality reveals which organizations are critical bridges—removing them would fragment the network.

nodes.title: Advanced Applications: Network Analysis in Social Work
nodes.content: Graph theory enables sophisticated social work analysis. Centrality measures (degree, betweenness, closeness, eigenvector) reveal influential individuals and organizations in service networks. Community detection algorithms identify clusters of closely collaborating agencies. Network resilience analysis shows whether losing one key organization would fragment service delivery. Stakeholder mapping uses graph visualization to clarify complex partnership ecosystems for intervention planning. In family systems work, structural analysis of genograms reveals unhealthy triangulation patterns and identifies supportive subsystems. For organizational development, network analysis guides strategic partner selection and identifies service gaps where new organizations are needed.

nodes.title: Advanced Network Mastery Achieved
nodes.content: You've demonstrated mastery of graph theory and its sophisticated applications in social work. You can now analyze complex networks, identify critical stakeholders, detect community structures, and design interventions based on network properties. Your ability to apply centrality measures, shortest path algorithms, and network resilience analysis positions you to lead network-based organizational change and research initiatives. Continue exploring advanced topics like dynamic networks (changing over time) and weighted networks (relationship strength varies).

nodes.title: Practice: Network Analysis in Social Work Scenarios
nodes.content: Apply your graph theory knowledge to realistic social work challenges.

questions.question: A food bank serves 6 neighborhoods. Currently, it's positioned in neighborhood A and serves all others, but clients in neighborhood F travel 45 minutes. Using graph theory, what would you recommend?
options: Increase staff at the central location
Identify a central location (minimize total travel distance for all neighborhoods)
feedback: This doesn't address the structural problem.
Correct! Graph theory's centrality concepts guide optimal location placement.
That abandons vulnerable populations.
Puts burden on clients rather than fixing the system.
questions.explanation: Using centrality measures and optimization algorithms helps identify service locations that minimize barriers for all clients.

questions.question: An organization notices that two program teams never collaborate or share referrals. Using network analysis, what might this indicate?
options: One team is better than the other
A disconnected component or weak bridge in the organization
feedback: Collaboration isn't about quality.
Correct! This is a structural network problem that needs addressing.
Capacity issues are separate from collaboration structure.
Many organizations overcome geographic constraints.
questions.explanation: Network analysis reveals structural disconnections that may indicate communication problems, siloed programs, or missing bridges.

questions.question: A client has no family support, limited friends, and recently quit their support group. What graph property is most concerning?
options: Being in a large connected component
feedback: That's not the primary concern here.
Correct! Isolation (low degree centrality) is a major social work risk factor.
That would actually be protective.
That measures local clustering, not isolation.
questions.explanation: Low degree centrality (few connections) indicates social isolation, a key risk factor requiring immediate intervention.

nodes.title: Competent Network Analysis Skills
nodes.content: You've achieved solid understanding of graph theory fundamentals and their application to social work practice. You can identify networks, count connections, understand basic centrality concepts, and apply these insights to real situations. You're ready to use network mapping tools, collaborate with organizational development specialists, and identify network-based interventions. Consider deepening your skills in specific areas like family systems analysis, organizational network mapping, or community partnership development.

nodes.title: Graph Basics: Simple Network Thinking for Social Work
nodes.content: Let's start simple: a graph is just a picture of who's connected to whom. Think of a client's support network: the client is in the middle, and draw lines to their parents, best friend, counselor, and faith leader. That's a graph! The people are 'nodes' (dots) and the relationships are 'edges' (lines). Now imagine a bigger picture: all the social service agencies in your city, with lines showing which ones send clients to each other. That's also a graph. Graphs help social workers see patterns that aren't obvious otherwise. For example, if you notice one organization is connected to everyone else, that organization is important—losing it would hurt the whole system. If you notice a client has very few connections, that client is isolated and needs support.

nodes.title: Worked Example: Mapping a Family Crisis Network
nodes.content: Maria is a 15-year-old referred to your agency by her school after multiple absences. Let's map her social support network. Maria has: her mother (supportive), father (absent), grandmother (very supportive, financial), older brother (removed from home), three school friends, school counselor, and a youth mentor from a community program. Nodes: Maria, mother, father, grandmother, brother, three friends, counselor, mentor. Edges: strong to mother and grandmother; weak/strained to father; moderate to friends; strong to counselor and mentor. Graph analysis shows: Maria has decent degree centrality (9 connections) but they're mostly peers or new relationships. Her strongest support is family (mother, grandmother). Recommendation: strengthen mentor relationships, help mother access respite care, potentially reconnect with father therapeutically.

nodes.title: Basic Graph Concepts Mastered
nodes.content: Excellent work! You now understand that graphs are networks of connections, nodes are entities (people, organizations, services), and edges are relationships. You recognize that social workers use graphs to map family systems, client networks, and organizational partnerships. You're ready to look at simple network diagrams, count connections, and spot isolated individuals or disconnected groups. As you encounter network thinking in your work, you'll build intuition for these patterns and gradually develop more sophisticated analysis skills.
